data.title: The Great Cheese Heist
data.premise: Serenity lands on a dusty moon for a simple cargo run, but Mal stumbles upon a ridiculous plot involving stolen government cheese. Now, he must navigate a web of hungry bureaucrats and cunning smugglers to turn a profit.
data.central_question: Can Mal pull off the heist of the century with a cargo hold full of cheese?

setting.name: Dusty Moon Colony
setting.description: A barren moon with a small colony struggling to survive. The colony is known for its makeshift markets and shady dealings. The air is dry, and the streets are filled with dust and the occasional stray goat.
setting.tone_notes: Lighthearted and comedic, with a touch of Western grit. Expect quirky characters and absurd situations.

acts.title: The Cheese Discovery
acts.description: Mal and the crew land on the moon for a routine cargo run but stumble upon a warehouse full of stolen government cheese. They meet a desperate bureaucrat who offers them a deal.
acts.end_condition: Mal agrees to help the bureaucrat steal the cheese.
locked_constraints: The cheese is not yet stolen
The crew does not know the full plan

acts.title: The Cheese Chase
acts.description: Mal and the crew execute their plan to steal the cheese, but they face unexpected challenges, including rival smugglers and a goat stampede.
acts.end_condition: The cheese is loaded onto Serenity.
locked_constraints: The cheese is not yet on Serenity
The rival smugglers are still active

acts.title: The Cheese Showdown
acts.description: Back in space, Mal and the crew must outwit Alliance patrols and decide what to do with their cheesy cargo. The bureaucrat reveals a surprising twist.
acts.end_condition: The fate of the cheese is decided.
locked_constraints: The cheese is not yet sold
The Alliance patrols are still searching

npcs.name: Simon Tam
npcs.secret: Knows a secret government recipe for cheese-based medicine

npcs.name: Jayne Cobb
npcs.secret: Has a hidden stash of cheese

factions.name: Alliance
factions.description: The governing body of the 'Verse, known for its strict regulations and bureaucratic red tape.

factions.name: Smugglers
factions.description: A loose network of independent operators who thrive on the fringes of the law.

world_rules: Cheese is a highly valuable commodity on this moon.
Alliance patrols are always watching for suspicious activity.
Goats are a common nuisance and can be unpredictable.

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it

- All four choices should feel plausible and interesting

- Focus on prose quality, atmosphere, character voice, and dramatic tension
- Do NOT include state_updates, health numbers, or mechanical data in your response
- Pace the story toward the current act's end condition. Check the CURRENT ACT PACING section for scene count and proximity
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes
- Introduce key NPCs (antagonists, allies, mentors) naturally throughout each act. Do not wait until the climax to introduce important characters. Check the NPC INTRODUCTION DIRECTIVES section if present.
Generate the opening passage for this story. Set the scene, establish the protagonist's situation, and present the first four choices. Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: You guide Serenity down onto the dusty moon's only landing pad, the ship's engines whining in protes

data.passage:
You guide Serenity down onto the dusty moon's only landing pad, the ship's engines whining in protest. The colony is a patchwork of rust and desperation under a pale sky. Dust swirls around your boots as you step onto the packed earth. Jayne Cobb is already at your side, sniffing the air like a bloodhound.

'What in the gorramn 'Verse is that stink?' he asks, wrinkling his nose. 'Smells like a dairy farm died and came back wrong.'

Simon Tam descends the ramp gracefully, his expression pained. 'It's likely aged cheese, Jayne. And given the regulatory signs on that warehouse, it's probably government-issue. We should avoid any unnecessary attention.'

Before you can reply, a commotion erupts near the warehouse. A flustered man in an ill-fitting Alliance uniform – Bureaucrat Bob, according to his badge – is waving his arms at a cloaked figure who just ducked inside. The figure turns for a moment, revealing the smirk of Rival Smuggler Rita, before vanishing into the shadows. The pungent odor from the warehouse grows stronger, mingling with the dust and tension.

A.text: Storm into the warehouse and demand to know what's so valuable it's causing all this fuss.
A.consequence: Potential confrontation with Rival Smuggler Rita and increased Alliance attention.
A.narration_directive: Describe the chaotic scene inside the warehouse as Mal confronts Rita and the Alliance.

B.text: Play it cool and approach Bureaucrat Bob to offer your 'services'.
B.consequence: Mal gains information from Bureaucrat Bob and avoids immediate conflict.
B.narration_directive: Show Mal negotiating with Bob, using his charm to extract useful details.

C.text: Have Jayne circle around to the back of the warehouse while you and Simon talk to Bob.
C.consequence: Jayne sneaks into the warehouse, potentially triggering a confrontation or discovery.
C.narration_directive: Depict Jayne's stealthy approach and the tension as he nears the warehouse.

D.text: Forget the cheese – stick to the original plan and find your cargo contact in the market.
D.consequence: Mal avoids the cheese situation entirely and focuses on the original cargo run.
D.narration_directive: Illustrate Mal's decision to stay on task and head to the market.

STYLE & TONE: A typical episode of Firefly, one of the lighthearted comedic episodes
The kind of consequences you create MUST fit this tone. Read what the player wrote and match it — the same mechanical severity plays very differently depending on the story's voice.

DIFFICULTY: Hard
- Game over allowed: true

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

This is the OPENING PASSAGE. No player choice was made yet. Do not apply any negative consequences or penalties — the player has not made any choices yet. time_elapsed should reflect the scene's timeframe.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You guide Serenity down onto the dusty moon's only landing pad, the ship's engines whining in protest. The colony is a patchwork of rust and desperation under a pale sky. Dust swirls around your boots as you step onto the packed earth. Jayne Cobb is already at your side, sniffing the air like a bloodhound.

'What in the gorramn 'Verse is that stink?' he asks, wrinkling his nose. 'Smells like a dairy farm died and came back wrong.'

Simon Tam descends the ramp gracefully, his expression pained. 'It's likely aged cheese, Jayne. And given the regulatory signs on that warehouse, it's probably government-issue. We should avoid any unnecessary attention.'

Before you can reply, a commotion erupts near the warehouse. A flustered man in an ill-fitting Alliance uniform – Bureaucrat Bob, according to his badge – is waving his arms at a cloaked figure who just ducked inside. The figure turns for a moment, revealing the smirk of Rival Smuggler Rita, before vanishing into the shadows. The pungent odor from the warehouse grows stronger, mingling with the dust and tension.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Storm into the warehouse and demand to know what's so valuable it's causing all this fuss.
B: Play it cool and approach Bureaucrat Bob to offer your 'services'.
C: Have Jayne circle around to the back of the warehouse while you and Simon talk to Bob.
D: Forget the cheese – stick to the original plan and find your cargo contact in the market.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: You shake your head, the rich, funky scent of cheese lingering in the dry air. 'We ain't here for a

data.passage:
You shake your head, the rich, funky scent of cheese lingering in the dry air. 'We ain't here for a gourmet tour,' you declare, turning your coat collar up against the dust. 'Original plan stands. Market.' Simon exhales softly, a hint of approval in his eyes. Jayne scratches his head, muttering, 'Smelled like money to me.'

The path to the market is a rutted track flanked by prefab huts. Stray goats nibble at discarded packaging, and the buzz of haggling voices grows louder. The market itself is a sprawling, dusty circus of makeshift stalls—everything from salvaged tech to dubious meat snacks. Simon stays close, his posture tense. Jayne’s eyes light up at a stall advertising 'untraceable' firearms.

You spot Garvin, your cargo contact, by a stall piled high with hydraulic parts. But movement catches your eye: a woman in worn leathers is calmly directing a dozen goats with short, sharp whistles. The animals move with uncanny discipline, and a familiar, potent aroma of aged cheese emanates from a large, tarp-covered cart beside her. Garvin waves you over, but the goat-herder meets your gaze, her expression cool and calculating.

A.text: Walk straight to Garvin and secure your cargo—no distractions.
A.consequence: Mal secures the cargo without complications.
A.narration_directive: Describe Mal smoothly handling the cargo deal with Garvin.

B.text: Approach the goat-herder. Anyone that organized with livestock knows more than she's letting on.
B.consequence: Mal learns about the goat-herder's connection to the cheese, but draws unwanted attention.
B.narration_directive: Show the goat-herder's guarded reaction and hint at her alliance with Rival Smuggler Rita.

C.text: Send Jayne to handle Garvin while you and Simon subtly investigate that cheese cart.
C.consequence: Mal and Simon gather intel on the cheese cart, but Jayne botches the cargo deal.
C.narration_directive: Depict Jayne's clumsy negotiation and Mal's frustration.

D.text: Blend into the crowd, listening for chatter about cheese shipments or unusual deals.
D.consequence: Mal overhears valuable information about the cheese shipment but loses time.
D.narration_directive: Reveal overheard details about the cheese's destination and the Alliance's interest.

    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",

  },

    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:

- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious setbacks and losses as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You shake your head, the rich, funky scent of cheese lingering in the dry air. 'We ain't here for a gourmet tour,' you declare, turning your coat collar up against the dust. 'Original plan stands. Market.' Simon exhales softly, a hint of approval in his eyes. Jayne scratches his head, muttering, 'Smelled like money to me.'

The path to the market is a rutted track flanked by prefab huts. Stray goats nibble at discarded packaging, and the buzz of haggling voices grows louder. The market itself is a sprawling, dusty circus of makeshift stalls—everything from salvaged tech to dubious meat snacks. Simon stays close, his posture tense. Jayne’s eyes light up at a stall advertising 'untraceable' firearms.

You spot Garvin, your cargo contact, by a stall piled high with hydraulic parts. But movement catches your eye: a woman in worn leathers is calmly directing a dozen goats with short, sharp whistles. The animals move with uncanny discipline, and a familiar, potent aroma of aged cheese emanates from a large, tarp-covered cart beside her. Garvin waves you over, but the goat-herder meets your gaze, her expression cool and calculating.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Walk straight to Garvin and secure your cargo—no distractions.
B: Approach the goat-herder. Anyone that organized with livestock knows more than she's letting on.
C: Send Jayne to handle Garvin while you and Simon subtly investigate that cheese cart.
D: Blend into the crowd, listening for chatter about cheese shipments or unusual deals.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passagePreview: You cut through the bustling market with purpose, Garvin's stall in sight. Simon matches your stride

data.passage: You cut through the bustling market with purpose, Garvin's stall in sight. Simon matches your stride, his doctor's bag swinging, while Jayne scowls at the crowd. Garvin, a wiry man with an eye patch and a no-nonsense demeanor, tips his hat. 'Mal. Credits upfront, cargo's in the back.' The exchange is quick and clean—your credits for his signature on the manifest. Simon allows himself a small, relieved smile. 'Efficient,' he notes. Jayne kicks at the dust. 'Efficient ain't profitable.' As you tuck the manifest away, that familiar, overpowering scent of aged cheese rolls in from the direction of a large, nondescript warehouse. Garvin follows your gaze and leans in, his voice low. 'Government issue. Went missing last night. Alliance's offering a finder's fee, but word is a smuggler named Rita's already got her eyes on it.' He gestures subtly toward a jittery man in a slightly rumpled Alliance suit—Bureaucrat Bob—pacing near some crates. Across the square, a sharp-featured woman in leather leans against a post, a smirk on her face and a few goats nibbling at her boots. She watches your crew with unmistakable interest.

A.text: Head straight for the cheese warehouse to see the loot for yourself.
A.consequence: Mal investigates the warehouse, attracting unwanted attention.
A.narration_directive: Describe Mal sneaking into the warehouse and encountering a guard.

B.text: Approach the pacing bureaucrat and hear what he has to say.
B.consequence: Mal talks to Bureaucrat Bob and learns more about the cheese.
B.narration_directive: Describe Mal approaching Bob and hearing his plea for help.

C.text: Confront the smirking woman with the goats—she looks like trouble.
C.consequence: Mal provokes Rita, leading to a confrontation with her goat army.
C.narration_directive: Describe Mal confronting Rita and triggering a goat stampede.

D.text: Return to Serenity immediately; this smells like more than just cheese.
D.consequence: Mal returns to Serenity to regroup with the crew.
D.narration_directive: Describe Mal deciding to leave the market and return to the ship.

{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

Pre-determined consequence: "Mal secures the cargo without complications."
Narration directive: "Describe Mal smoothly handling the cargo deal with Garvin."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative consequences. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious setbacks and losses as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You cut through the bustling market with purpose, Garvin's stall in sight. Simon matches your stride, his doctor's bag swinging, while Jayne scowls at the crowd. Garvin, a wiry man with an eye patch and a no-nonsense demeanor, tips his hat. 'Mal. Credits upfront, cargo's in the back.' The exchange is quick and clean—your credits for his signature on the manifest. Simon allows himself a small, relieved smile. 'Efficient,' he notes. Jayne kicks at the dust. 'Efficient ain't profitable.' As you tuck the manifest away, that familiar, overpowering scent of aged cheese rolls in from the direction of a large, nondescript warehouse. Garvin follows your gaze and leans in, his voice low. 'Government issue. Went missing last night. Alliance's offering a finder's fee, but word is a smuggler named Rita's already got her eyes on it.' He gestures subtly toward a jittery man in a slightly rumpled Alliance suit—Bureaucrat Bob—pacing near some crates. Across the square, a sharp-featured woman in leather leans against a post, a smirk on her face and a few goats nibbling at her boots. She watches your crew with unmistakable interest.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Head straight for the cheese warehouse to see the loot for yourself.
B: Approach the pacing bureaucrat and hear what he has to say.
C: Confront the smirking woman with the goats—she looks like trouble.
D: Return to Serenity immediately; this smells like more than just cheese.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: You decide that the market's cheese intrigue is more than you bargained for. With a firm nod to Simo

data.passage:
You decide that the market's cheese intrigue is more than you bargained for. With a firm nod to Simon and Jayne, you say, 'Back to the ship. Now.'

Simon breathes a sigh of relief. 'Good. The sooner we're off this rock, the better.'

Jayne scowls but falls in step. 'Ain't no profit in runnin', Mal.'

The walk back to Serenity is short but tense. The colony's dust swirls around your boots, and the midday heat makes your coat feel heavy. As you round the corner to the landing pad, you see a woman leaning casually against Serenity's cargo ramp. She's dressed in practical smuggler's gear, and her eyes lock onto you with a knowing glint.

'Malcolm Reynolds,' she says, her voice smooth but edged. 'I've been expecting you. My name's Rita. Heard you're in the market for some... cheesy opportunities.' She flashes a grin that doesn't reach her eyes.

Simon exchanges a worried glance with you, while Jayne's hand hovers near his pistol. The air crackles with unspoken threats, and you know this encounter could turn sour faster than milk in the sun.

A.text: Demand to know what Rita wants and how she found you.
A.consequence: Mal learns Rita's intentions but risks escalating tension.
A.narration_directive: Describe Mal confronting Rita and her revealing her knowledge of the cheese heist.

B.text: Brush past Rita and order the crew to prepare for takeoff.
B.consequence: Mal avoids conflict but misses an opportunity to gather information.
B.narration_directive: Describe Mal brushing past Rita and ordering the crew to prepare for takeoff.

C.text: Play along with Rita to gather information about the cheese heist.
C.consequence: Mal gains valuable information about the cheese heist but risks Rita's trust.
C.narration_directive: Describe Mal playing along with Rita and uncovering her plans.

D.text: Signal Jayne to distract Rita while you and Simon board the ship.
D.consequence: Mal's attempt to evade Rita fails, leading to a confrontation.
D.narration_directive: Describe Jayne's distraction attempt backfiring and Rita confronting Mal directly.

- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option D.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Mal returns to Serenity to regroup with the crew."
Narration directive: "Describe Mal deciding to leave the market and return to the ship."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative consequences. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious setbacks and losses as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You decide that the market's cheese intrigue is more than you bargained for. With a firm nod to Simon and Jayne, you say, 'Back to the ship. Now.'

Simon breathes a sigh of relief. 'Good. The sooner we're off this rock, the better.'

Jayne scowls but falls in step. 'Ain't no profit in runnin', Mal.'

The walk back to Serenity is short but tense. The colony's dust swirls around your boots, and the midday heat makes your coat feel heavy. As you round the corner to the landing pad, you see a woman leaning casually against Serenity's cargo ramp. She's dressed in practical smuggler's gear, and her eyes lock onto you with a knowing glint.

'Malcolm Reynolds,' she says, her voice smooth but edged. 'I've been expecting you. My name's Rita. Heard you're in the market for some... cheesy opportunities.' She flashes a grin that doesn't reach her eyes.

Simon exchanges a worried glance with you, while Jayne's hand hovers near his pistol. The air crackles with unspoken threats, and you know this encounter could turn sour faster than milk in the sun.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Demand to know what Rita wants and how she found you.
B: Brush past Rita and order the crew to prepare for takeoff.
C: Play along with Rita to gather information about the cheese heist.
D: Signal Jayne to distract Rita while you and Simon board the ship.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passagePreview: You give Rival Smuggler Rita a dismissive wave and brush past her, your worn coat swirling in the mi

data.passage:
You give Rival Smuggler Rita a dismissive wave and brush past her, your worn coat swirling in the midday dust. 'Ain't got time for your brand of trouble today,' you say, not bothering to look back as she chuckles softly. 'Your loss, Captain. That cheese won't steal itself.' Her words hang in the air, but you're already moving.

Simon falls into step beside you, his brow furrowed. 'She was clearly fishing for information, Mal. We might have learned something useful.' Jayne spits near Rita's retreating boots. 'Smelled like a setup. Let's get off this rock before the Alliance sniffs around.'

You stride up Serenity's ramp, raising your voice. 'Kaylee! Fire up the engines. We're leaving.' A cheerful 'On it, Cap'n!' echoes from the engine room. As Simon heads to the med bay and Jayne starts checking cargo straps, a frantic figure in a wrinkled Alliance suit stumbles onto the landing pad, waving arms like a distressed bird.

'Captain Reynolds! Wait!' Bureaucrat Bob huffs up to the ramp, face flushed. 'I need to speak with you urgently—about the cheese. I have a proposition, and it's worth your while.' Simon peers down from the hatch. 'His pulse is racing. He's either desperate or lying.' Kaylee's voice crackles over the comm: 'Got a little hiccup in the compression coil. Need about ten minutes, Cap'n.' You stand at the threshold, the hum of Serenity around you.

A.text: Invite Bureaucrat Bob aboard to hear his proposition properly.
A.consequence: Mal hears Bob's proposition, potentially gaining valuable information but risking Alliance attention.
A.narration_directive: Describe Bob's nervous energy as he lays out his plan, with Simon monitoring for deception.

B.text: Shout at Kaylee to hurry the repairs and ignore Bob's pleas.
B.consequence: Mal prioritizes leaving quickly, avoiding potential trouble but missing Bob's offer.
B.narration_directive: Show Mal's impatience as he barks orders, with Bob's protests fading into the background.

C.text: Ask Simon to quietly assess if Bob is telling the truth before deciding.
C.consequence: Simon's assessment reveals whether Bob is trustworthy, giving Mal an edge in negotiations.
C.narration_directive: Focus on Simon's subtle examination of Bob, with Mal watching for tells.

D.text: Have Jayne escort Bob off the pad while you check on the engine flutter.
D.consequence: Jayne's rough handling of Bob angers the bureaucrat, damaging future relations with the Alliance.
D.narration_directive: Detail Jayne's aggressive escort and Bob's furious threats as he's removed from the pad.

    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option B.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Mal avoids conflict but misses an opportunity to gather information."
Narration directive: "Describe Mal brushing past Rita and ordering the crew to prepare for takeoff."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative consequences. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious setbacks and losses as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You give Rival Smuggler Rita a dismissive wave and brush past her, your worn coat swirling in the midday dust. 'Ain't got time for your brand of trouble today,' you say, not bothering to look back as she chuckles softly. 'Your loss, Captain. That cheese won't steal itself.' Her words hang in the air, but you're already moving.

Simon falls into step beside you, his brow furrowed. 'She was clearly fishing for information, Mal. We might have learned something useful.' Jayne spits near Rita's retreating boots. 'Smelled like a setup. Let's get off this rock before the Alliance sniffs around.'

You stride up Serenity's ramp, raising your voice. 'Kaylee! Fire up the engines. We're leaving.' A cheerful 'On it, Cap'n!' echoes from the engine room. As Simon heads to the med bay and Jayne starts checking cargo straps, a frantic figure in a wrinkled Alliance suit stumbles onto the landing pad, waving arms like a distressed bird.

'Captain Reynolds! Wait!' Bureaucrat Bob huffs up to the ramp, face flushed. 'I need to speak with you urgently—about the cheese. I have a proposition, and it's worth your while.' Simon peers down from the hatch. 'His pulse is racing. He's either desperate or lying.' Kaylee's voice crackles over the comm: 'Got a little hiccup in the compression coil. Need about ten minutes, Cap'n.' You stand at the threshold, the hum of Serenity around you.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Invite Bureaucrat Bob aboard to hear his proposition properly.
B: Shout at Kaylee to hurry the repairs and ignore Bob's pleas.
C: Ask Simon to quietly assess if Bob is telling the truth before deciding.
D: Have Jayne escort Bob off the pad while you check on the engine flutter.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You bark at Kaylee, "Hurry it up, darlin'! We ain't got all day!" She scurries under the engine, tools clanking in a symphony of haste. Bureaucrat Bob waves his arms frantically, his pleas about stolen cheese and bureaucratic nightmares blending into the dusty wind. You turn your back, deliberately ignoring him.

Simon stands nearby, looking concerned. He smooths his jacket and says, "Mal, ignoring a representative of the Alliance could have repercussions. He might be offering a legitimate opportunity."

Jayne chuckles, leaning against a cargo crate. "Opportunity for what? A headache? Let the fool talk to himself. We got places to be."

Bob's voice fades into the background as Kaylee pops out, wiping grease from her hands. "Repairs are done, Cap'n! Good as new!" You feel a surge of satisfaction—no trouble, no complications. But as you're about to order the crew aboard Serenity, a loud commotion erupts near the warehouse. Shouts echo, accompanied by the clatter of crates and that unmistakable, pungent aroma of cheese wafting through the air. A crowd gathers, and you spot several rough-looking individuals hauling boxes from the building.

A.text: Head towards the warehouse to see what all the fuss is about.
A.consequence: Mal investigates the warehouse, risking entanglement with the smugglers.
A.narration_directive: Describe Mal cautiously approaching the warehouse, noting the suspicious activity and the growing tension.

B.text: Forget the noise and get Serenity off the ground. Now.
B.consequence: Mal avoids the commotion and focuses on leaving the moon.
B.narration_directive: Show Mal's determination to leave, with the crew scrambling to board Serenity as the commotion continues in the background.

C.text: Send Jayne to check out the commotion. He's good at causing trouble, might as well use him.
C.consequence: Jayne causes trouble, alerting the smugglers and complicating the situation.
C.narration_directive: Depict Jayne's reckless approach, leading to a confrontation with the smugglers and escalating the danger.

D.text: Sigh and turn back to Bob. Maybe he knows something worth hearing.
D.consequence: Mal learns valuable information from Bob, gaining an advantage.
D.narration_directive: Show Mal reluctantly engaging with Bob, who reveals crucial details about the cheese heist.

{
  "state_updates": {

    },

    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option B.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Mal prioritizes leaving quickly, avoiding potential trouble but missing Bob's offer."
Narration directive: "Show Mal's impatience as he barks orders, with Bob's protests fading into the background."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative consequences. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious setbacks and losses as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You bark at Kaylee, "Hurry it up, darlin'! We ain't got all day!" She scurries under the engine, tools clanking in a symphony of haste. Bureaucrat Bob waves his arms frantically, his pleas about stolen cheese and bureaucratic nightmares blending into the dusty wind. You turn your back, deliberately ignoring him.

Simon stands nearby, looking concerned. He smooths his jacket and says, "Mal, ignoring a representative of the Alliance could have repercussions. He might be offering a legitimate opportunity."

Jayne chuckles, leaning against a cargo crate. "Opportunity for what? A headache? Let the fool talk to himself. We got places to be."

Bob's voice fades into the background as Kaylee pops out, wiping grease from her hands. "Repairs are done, Cap'n! Good as new!" You feel a surge of satisfaction—no trouble, no complications. But as you're about to order the crew aboard Serenity, a loud commotion erupts near the warehouse. Shouts echo, accompanied by the clatter of crates and that unmistakable, pungent aroma of cheese wafting through the air. A crowd gathers, and you spot several rough-looking individuals hauling boxes from the building.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Head towards the warehouse to see what all the fuss is about.
B: Forget the noise and get Serenity off the ground. Now.
C: Send Jayne to check out the commotion. He's good at causing trouble, might as well use him.
D: Sigh and turn back to Bob. Maybe he knows something worth hearing.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: You don't waste a second. 'Everyone onboard! Now!' you bark, your voice cutting through the dusty ai

data.passage:
You don't waste a second. 'Everyone onboard! Now!' you bark, your voice cutting through the dusty air. The commotion from the cheese warehouse is growing—shouts, the bleating of goats, and the distinct smell of panic. But you've got a ship to fly and a cargo to deliver. Simon is already at the ramp, ushering Kaylee and the others inside with practiced efficiency. Jayne, however, lingers, his eyes narrowed at the chaos. 'Smells like opportunity, Mal,' he grumbles, but a sharp look from you sends him shuffling up the ramp.

Serenity's engines whine to life, a comforting hum that promises escape from this cheese-scented nightmare. You slide into the pilot's seat, hands on the controls. Through the cockpit window, you see a cloud of dust approaching—a figure running frantically from the warehouse. It's Bureaucrat Bob, his Alliance uniform disheveled, waving his arms like a madman. Behind him, a trio of goats break free from a pen and start stampeding toward the landing pad.

Simon leans in from the doorway. 'Mal, we have company. And livestock.'

You're moments from lift-off, but Bob is directly in your path, and those goats are adding to the chaos. The Alliance patrols might be alerted by now. Your grip tightens on the controls.

A.text: Punch it! We're leaving, and if Bob's in the way, that's his problem.
A.consequence: Mal risks damaging Serenity or running over Bureaucrat Bob, potentially alerting Alliance patrols.
A.narration_directive: Describe the chaotic takeoff, with goats scattering and Bob narrowly avoiding disaster.

B.text: Hold position. Let's see what the bureaucrat wants—might be profitable.
B.consequence: Mal hears Bob out, potentially gaining information or a new deal.
B.narration_directive: Show Bob's desperation as he pleads with Mal, with the goats causing havoc in the background.

C.text: Jayne, get out there and clear the path. Use your charm or your fists.
C.consequence: Jayne's 'charm' escalates the situation, attracting more attention from Alliance patrols.
C.narration_directive: Depict Jayne's brutal handling of the goats and Bob, leading to a louder commotion.

D.text: Simon, open a channel to the Alliance. Time to play the good citizen.
D.consequence: Mal's attempt to play the good citizen backfires, resulting in Serenity being seized by the Alliance.
D.narration_directive: Show the Alliance patrols arriving swiftly, confiscating Serenity and arresting the crew.

    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },

    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative consequences or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the tone (see STYLE & TONE above). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- Consequences are severe by default. When the player makes a risky choice, the outcome MUST hurt — lost allies, destroyed resources, permanent relationship damage. Do NOT hedge or soften.
- Dangerous situations MUST feel dangerous: at least one choice per set SHOULD carry severe_penalty or worse when the narrative context warrants it.
- game_over outcomes are expected. Use them when the narrative situation creates genuine mortal/critical danger and the player walks into it. Do not gate game_over behind artificial rarity — let the story dictate when death or failure is on the table.
- NPCs hold grudges. A betrayal or major insult MUST cause lasting relationship damage (-30 or worse). Reconciliation requires significant effort.
- Recovery from severe setbacks requires sacrifice — time, resources, or relationships spent to undo damage.
- narration_directive: be specific and unflinching. Tell the Writer exactly what was lost, broken, or destroyed. No euphemisms.

- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious setbacks and losses as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You don't waste a second. 'Everyone onboard! Now!' you bark, your voice cutting through the dusty air. The commotion from the cheese warehouse is growing—shouts, the bleating of goats, and the distinct smell of panic. But you've got a ship to fly and a cargo to deliver. Simon is already at the ramp, ushering Kaylee and the others inside with practiced efficiency. Jayne, however, lingers, his eyes narrowed at the chaos. 'Smells like opportunity, Mal,' he grumbles, but a sharp look from you sends him shuffling up the ramp.

Serenity's engines whine to life, a comforting hum that promises escape from this cheese-scented nightmare. You slide into the pilot's seat, hands on the controls. Through the cockpit window, you see a cloud of dust approaching—a figure running frantically from the warehouse. It's Bureaucrat Bob, his Alliance uniform disheveled, waving his arms like a madman. Behind him, a trio of goats break free from a pen and start stampeding toward the landing pad.

Simon leans in from the doorway. 'Mal, we have company. And livestock.'

You're moments from lift-off, but Bob is directly in your path, and those goats are adding to the chaos. The Alliance patrols might be alerted by now. Your grip tightens on the controls.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Punch it! We're leaving, and if Bob's in the way, that's his problem.
B: Hold position. Let's see what the bureaucrat wants—might be profitable.
C: Jayne, get out there and clear the path. Use your charm or your fists.
D: Simon, open a channel to the Alliance. Time to play the good citizen.

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn
